coredata.dc:title: Lysosome-targetable brightly green fluorescence carbon dots for real-time monitoring in cell and highly efficient removal in environment of hypochlorite

coredata.dc:description: Due to the lacks of lysosome localization group and reaction/interaction site for hypochlorite (ClO-) on the surface of the carbon dots (C-dots), no C-dots-based lysosome-targeted fluorescence probes have, so far, been reported for real-time monitoring intracellular ClO-. In this work, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) was used as a precursor to prepare C-dots with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 485 and 532 nm, respectively, and quantum yield ∼ 27% by a hydrothermal approach at 196 °C for 6 h under a reductive atmosphere. The brightly green C-dots can sensitively and quickly respond to ClO- in aqueous solution through surface chemical reaction, showing a linear relationship in the range of 0.5–120 μΜ ClO- with 0.27 μΜ of limit of detection (LOD). Most significantly, the C-dots can localize at intracellular lysosome to image ClO- in lysosomes. Also, the magnetic nanocomposites (C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs) were fabricated via a simple electrostatic self-assembly between Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 MNPs) and C-dots for highly efficient removal of ClO- in real samples. Therefore, lysosome-targetable C-dots-based probes for real-time monitoring ClO- were successfully constructed, opening up a promising door to investigate the biological functions and pathological roles of ClO- at organelle levels.

head.ce:title: Lysosome-targetable brightly green fluorescence carbon dots for real-time monitoring in cell and highly efficient removal in environment of hypochlorite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the lacks of lysosome localization group and reaction/interaction site for hypochlorite (ClO-) on the surface of the carbon dots (C-dots), no C-dots-based lysosome-targeted fluorescence probes have, so far, been reported for real-time monitoring intracellular ClO-. In this work, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) was used as a precursor to prepare C-dots with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 485 and 532 nm, respectively, and quantum yield ∼ 27% by a hydrothermal approach at 196 °C for 6 h under a reductive atmosphere. The brightly green C-dots can sensitively and quickly respond to ClO- in aqueous solution through surface chemical reaction, showing a linear relationship in the range of 0.5–120 μΜ ClO- with 0.27 μΜ of limit of detection (LOD). Most significantly, the C-dots can localize at intracellular lysosome to image ClO- in lysosomes. Also, the magnetic nanocomposites (C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs) were fabricated via a simple electrostatic self-assembly between Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 MNPs) and C-dots for highly efficient removal of ClO- in real samples. Therefore, lysosome-targetable C-dots-based probes for real-time monitoring ClO- were successfully constructed, opening up a promising door to investigate the biological functions and pathological roles of ClO- at organelle levels.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite (ClO-), as one of the most important reactive oxygen species (ROS), plays an important role in various physiological processes such as signal transduction, differentiation, migration, and cellular immunity [1]. Endogenous ClO- is produced by hydrogen peroxide oxidizing chloride ion catalyzed by a heme enzyme myeloperoxidase [2], which not only defends against the bacteria and inflammation, but also acts as a natural adjuvant of adaptive immunity [3,4]. However, abnormal levels of ClO- may lead to atherosclerosis, rheumatoid, lung injury, cardiovascular disease, joint inflammation, and neuronal degeneration [5–7]. In particular, most of the resultant tissue damage is highly associated with atherosclerosis, neurodegeneration, Alzheimer’s disease, and even cancer [8]. Lysosome, one of the most essential organelles in eukaryotic cells, plays a vital role in cellular processes such as macromolecular decomposition [9]. The ClO- level is tightly related to the redox balance in lysosome, which is of great significance to maintain the normal lysosomal function. Lysosomal dysfunction causes neurodegenerative diseases, glucose metabolism dysfunction, and immune system diseases [10]. Studies have shown that ClO- can induce cell apoptosis through the rupture of lysosomes activated by the calcium dependent calpain [11,12]. Therefore, the real-time and effective monitoring of ClO- in lysosome is particularly meaningful and valuable.

ClO- is usually used as a sanitizer with a microbicidal efficacy high to 99.99% in the field of environment. The principle lies in the fact that the hydrolysis of ClO- produces atom oxygen that possesses a superstrong oxidizability, resulting in the protein denaturation of virus or pathogen. Moreover, the ClO- can cause the dangerousness in corrosivity and ambustion after long-term exposure in ClO- aqueous solution [13,14]. During the water treatment, the excessive ClO- dosage not only enables chloride ion concentration to go up, negatively influencing on the corrosion mitigation of water quality, but also affects pH equilibrium, leading to the exorbitant pH value [15]. Moreover, long-term and extensive usage of ClO- may pollute and damage the underground water, soil and air that mankind survives on. The residual and constant accumulation of ClO- utilized for the sterilization in public district may give rise to secondary pollution to food, especially for unpackaged one, bringing hidden danger to food safety [16,17]. Therefore, it is essential to develop strategies for highly efficient removal of ClO- in environment.

Fluorescence approaches have been widely used as a robust and effective tool for detecting ClO- owing to the noninvasiveness, visual visualization, simple operation, and rapid response [8,18,19]. Till now, tremendous efforts have been attracted for the development of dye-based or upconversion nanoparticles-based fluorescent probes for the detection and intracellular imaging of ClO- [20–25]. Due to their high reproducibility and sensitivity, it is still highly desired to develop other novel fluorescent probes for better understanding of the origins, activities, and functions of ClO- in cell. In recent decade, carbon dots (C-dots) have been developed as an emerging and promising fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials [26–34], possessing a low toxicity, simple synthesis and purification, good photostability, excellent water solubility, and so on [35–38]. They have been successfully applied to determine ClO- in aqueous solution and image ClO- in cell [39–42]. Also, C-dots can realize the above-mentioned function through the modifications of interaction sites with ClO- [43,44]. Moreover, a few of C-dots have been reported can localize in subcellular lysosome for imaging [45], polarity detection [46], photothermal therapy [47], formaldehyde sensing [48], selective detection of permanganate and pH [49] and Fe(III) [50] in vivo and in vitro. Unfortunately, no lysosome-targeted fluorescent probes have, so far, been reported for the detection and imaging of ClO- in lysosomes. The possible reasons are the lacks of the lysosome localization group and reaction/interaction site of ClO- on the surface of the C-dots [51], which is still a challenge.

Herein, a one-step hydrothermal approach was adopted to fabricate brightly green C-dots using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) as a precursor under a reductive atmosphere (NaBH4 as a reductant). The C-dots can emit green fluorescence with excitation and emission at 485 nm and 532 nm, respectively, and ∼ 27% of quantum yield (QY). In consideration of the excellent photo-physicochemical properties, the C-dots were performed to detect ClO- in aqueous solution, exhibiting a sensitive and quick response to ClO-. Most interestingly, the C-dots can localize at intracellular lysosome, which realized the real-time detection and imaging of intracellular ClO-. Further, we synthesized Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposites decorated by C-dots (C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs) through electrostatic interaction between C-dots and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticle (Fe3O4 MNPs) for the highly efficient removal of the free ClO- in environmental samples. The mechanisms of fluorescence quenching and lysosome localization were considered.

1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP, over 99%) were the products of Yuhao Chemical Reagent Co., China. 3-Aminopropylphosphonic acid (3-APPA, 94%) were purchased from Alpha Aesar Co., China. All other reagents were of analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., China, and used as received. Dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 1 000) was acquired from Shanghai Green Bird S&T Co., China. Doubly deionized water (DDW) was prepared in Elix 5 pure water system (Millipore Inc., USA).

TNP as a precursor was adopted to prepare C-dots. Briefly, 0.1 g of yellow TNP and 0.076 g of NaBH4 were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water by ultrasonication for 1.0 h under an inert atmosphere. The resultant suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 196 °C for 6 h. After cooled to room temperature, the product containing water-soluble C-dots was filtered through a 0.22-mm microporous membrane to remove large precipitates, and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 2 d to remove soluble salt and free small molecules. The purified C-dots were preserved in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: According to our previous report with a slight modification, we prepared the Fe3O4 MNPs [52]. Briefly, 2 mmol of FeCl3 and 12 mL DDW were placed into a 50 mL flask. After the solution was ultrasonicated under nitrogen for 10 min, 1 mmol of FeCl2 and 0.125 mmol of 3-APPA were added to form transparent solution. Subsequently, 5 mL of 1.5 M NaOH solution was quickly injected into the flask. Then the resultant mixture was heated to 80◦C for the growth of nanoparticles for 1.0 h. The solution was finally cooled to room temperature and the resulting Fe3O4 nanoparticles were magnetic separation and repeatedly washed in turn with DDW and ethanol.

C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs were synthesized through electrostatic self-assembly between C-dots and Fe3O4 MNPs. In brief, 10 mg of C-dots was added into10 mL of 1.0 mg/mL Fe3O4 MNPs aqueous solution, and then ultrasonication for 30 min. The mixed solution was washed for 3–4 times by DDW to remove the unreacted C-dots. The production was placed into oven at 80 °C overnight for dry. The obtained light black powder was preserved in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The cytotoxicity of the C-dots was evaluated by standard MTT assay on HeLa, A549 and HepG2 cells. Typically, 1 × 104 cells per mL were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C with 95% relative humidity and 5% CO2. After 12 h, the cell medium was sucked out and the cells were charged with different concentrations of the C-dots and further incubated for 24 h. Later, the cells were incubated with MTT (20 μL, 0.5 mg/mL) for 2 h under a humidified condition, the absorbance of the solution was measured at 490 nm by a microplate reader (680, Bio-Rad Inc., USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lysosome localization and imaging of ClO- in lysosome
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were washed with PBS thoroughly and charged with ∼ 50 μg/mL of the C-dots in culture medium. The cells were kept under humidified conditions for 6 h. Next, the cells were washed with PBS solution again. The cells produced green fluorescence centred at 500–530 nm when excited at 450 nm. Overlap images were also obtained between the C-dots and the Lyso-Tracker Red by the corresponding luminescent intensities within the selected regions of interest. To conduct the colocalization experiments, the cells were treated with ∼ 50 μg/mL of the C-dots and incubated for 6 h. 10 μM of Lyso-Tracker Red was added just before cell imaging. The cells were excited at 450 nm for the C-dots, whereas the Lyso Tracker Red-treated cells were excited at 560 nm. Similarly, for imaging the intracellular ClO-, both the excess C-dots and Lyso-Tracker were removed by repeatedly washing the cells with PBS. Then culture medium and 200 μM ClO- were added into the dish. The fluorescence images were taken using a confocal microscope after washing with PBS.

The as-prepared C-dots were characterized in detail (Apparatus and characterizations in Supplementary Information (SI)). The survey curve of XPS spectrum reveals that the C-dots composed of three elements: carbon (∼285.1 eV), nitrogen (∼399.2 eV) and oxygen (∼531.9 eV), and the corresponding molecular weight ratios (wt%) are 77.7%, 12.41% and 9.89%, respectively (Fig. 1 A). As shown in Fig. 1B, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum displays three peaks at ∼ 284.7, ∼285.8, and ∼ 288.2 eV, which can be assigned to C-C/C = C, C-N, and C-OH groups, respectively [53]. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum exhibits three peaks with binding energy at ∼ 399.4, ∼400.5, and ∼ 406.0 eV, which are identified as the amine N, alkyl N, and nitryl N, respectively (Fig. 1C) [54,55]. The signals of –NO2 at ∼ 532.9 eV and –OH at ∼ 531.4 eV are visible on the high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D). In comparison to the molecular weight ratios (wt%) of C (56.98%), N (12.46%), and O (28.46) of TNP, the concentration reaction might occur among pyrene rings and the O of –NO2 was reduced by atomic H from NaBH4 to form water in the carbonization process. The decreased mass of O was almost remedied by that of C. These results suggest that the edge sites of the C-dots were functionalized by –OH, –NH2 and –NO2 groups, and the –NH2 groups were predominant (Scheme 1 A).

The FT-IR spectra were conducted to further confirm the functional groups of the C-dots revealed by XPS and demonstrate the possible other ones. The characteristic absorption peaks of O-H/N-H and C-H at 3415 and 620 cm−1, respectively, were observed on the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1E). With regard to the O-H signal, since the samples were fully dried before the FT-IR test, this result indicates the existence of hydroxyl group on the surface of C-dots [56]. This was further confirmed by the vibration of C-OH at 1274 cm−1 in the FT-IR curve. Similarly, the absorption peak of –NH2 at 1435 cm−1 was observed. Thus, the O-H and –NH2 signals were mainly ascribed to the hydroxyl and amine functionalization of C-dots. In addition, a strong vibration at 1565 cm−1 was ascribed to the aromatic C = C bonds [54]. The peak at 1350 cm−1 could be attributed to the –NO2 characteristic vibration [56], which was the unreduced nitryl group of TNP.

Also, the Zeta-potential, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive spectrum (EDS), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were used to characterize the surface charge, composition, morphology, and crystalline state of the C-dots. The −9.6 mV of Zeta-potential of C-dots indicates the highly negatively charged surface of the C-dots (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S2A, the C-dots nanoparticles were of a monodispersion state, exhibiting a nearly spherical morphology and had a diameter ranging from 2.2 to 5.3 nm with an average size of 3.5 nm. High-resolution TEM reveals that C-dots have (102) lattice spacing of graphitic carbon of 0.22 nm (Fig. S2B). The element mapping of the selected area of C-dots reveals that the C-dots composed of three elements of C, N, and O with a corresponding composition ratio (wt%) of 58.92%, 11.39%, and 35.93%, respectively (Fig. S2C). Compared with those of XPS, except for N element, the main difference of the other two elements possibly came from two factors: one was the background, the other was the sample differentiation. The XRD pattern of the C-dots shows a broad diffraction peak at 26° and a shoulder peak at 41° (Fig. 1F), indicating an interlayer spacing of 0.36 nm close to bulk graphite. This was possibly ascribed to the functionalization of C-OH and aromatic ring concentration [57,58].

The UV–vis absorption, maximum excitation and emission spectra, excitation- and pH-dependent emission, QY, and lifetime of the C-dots in aqueous solution were examined. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the normalized UV–vis curve displays two adsorption peaks: one at ∼ 295 nm probably originated from the π → π* transition of the carbon cores, the other around ∼ 340 nm was attributed to the n → π* transition of nitrogen atom of amine. In particular, the TNP-based C-dots had full visible band absorption, and the absorption redshift over 340 nm can be ascribed to the N-doped and O-containing functional groups at the edges [59,60]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the C-dots located at ∼ 485 and ∼ 532 nm, respectively. The main excitation bands were not well consistent with the corresponding excitonic absorption bands, suggesting that the emission was characterized by defect-state decay [54]. Accordingly, the brightly green fluorescence was observed under the irradiation of 365-nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. 2A). The quantum yield (QY) of the C-dots was determined to be ∼ 27% using quinine sulfate as a reference (Fig. 2B). The decay curve shown in Fig. 2C can be fitted to be a tri-exponential function, and the mean lifetime was ∼ 4.2 ns, suggesting the presence of three decay channels responsible for the observed photoluminescence pattern.

Also, the excitation- and pH- dependent emission behaviors were evaluated. Fig. 2D shows that the emission was synchronously enhanced and peak location barely shifted while the excitation wavelength was increased from 370 to 480 nm. However, with the sequentially enlarged excitation wavelength, the emission decreased along with a slight redshift. This result indicates that the C-dots were nearly excitation-independent. This might be attributed to the high crystallinity of the C-dots with few defects [55]. Owing to carbonization under the highly reductive atmosphere, the nitryl groups were reduced to amine ones and anchored at the edge of the C-dots. In addition, water was possibly involved in the redox reaction to form –C–OH [61]. These led to the varying isoelectric points and dissociation constants of the C-dots similar to ampholytes [62,63]. The pH-dependent emission behavior was observed (Fig. 2E). Thus, a stable emission platform appeared at pH > 6.0 (Fig. 2F). The visible excitation and green emission, relatively high QY, and excitation (370–490 nm)- and pH (6.0–12.0)-independent emission behaviors endowed the C-dots with a great potential for sensing and bio-imaging applications.

An abnormal level or fluctuation of ClO- may form byproducts responsible for the reproductive failure, renal disease, atherosclerosis, and cancer [43,64], even interrupt intracellular homeostasis, resulting in neurodegenerative/cardiovascular disease and diabetes mellitus [65]. Thus, it is highly desired to develop sensitive and facile response sensors for detecting ClO-. Considering the edge of the C-dots modified by amine groups, the C-dots were thus designed as a ClO- sensor. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when ClO- solution was injected into the C-dots solution (12 mg/mL), the fluorescence was gradually quenched. ClO- at 120 μΜ completely quenched the fluorescence of the C-dots. The images taken before and after dosing ClO- into the C-dots solution clearly exhibited a color change (inset in Fig. 3A). The quenching ratio (F0/F) displayed a linear relationship in the range of 0.5–120 μΜ ClO-. The LOD was calculated as 0.27 μM. The linear equation (1) was fitted as follows:
(1)
Y = 0.2159 C + 1.01324 ,
R
2
= 0.9974
where Y is the quenching ratio F0/F, F0 and F are the original and instant fluorescence intensity at 532 nm, respectively, C is the ClO- concentration (Fig. 3B). The other ROS species such as hydroxyl radical (OH.), singlet state oxygen (1O2), superoxide anion (.O2 –) and H2O2 were examined as interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 3C, except for OH., 1O2,.O2 – and H2O2 had no effect on the detection of ClO-. OH. slightly influenced the ClO- detection. Fortunately, OH. could be easily eliminated by its specific scavenger such as mannitol [66], which had no interference to the fluorescence of the C-dots in the presence of ClO-. Except for OH., the E θ
ClO - /Cl - is greater than those of other ROS, the good selectivity of chemical reaction-based sensor toward ClO- over other ROS can be explained. The oxidizability of OH. is the strongest but non-selective, which can mineralize all organics that were firstly touched [52]. Possibly, OH. got in touch with other groups on the surface of C-dots and then oxided them. Therefore, OH. exhibits little effect on the detection of ClO-. Compared with the recent reports about ClO- detection (Table S1), our C-dots displayed a generic sensing ability. However, our platform featured much simpler preparation, lower cost, etc., than the reports, especially in the lysosome localization and highly efficient removal of ClO- (vide post).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of C-dots to ClO-
ce:section.ce:para: It is essential to understand the mechanism for the optical response of the C-dots to ClO-. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the C-dots before and after the treatment with ClO- were compared (Fig. 4 A). The π-π* transition at ∼ 360 nm and n-π* transition at ∼ 280 nm, other two new broad absorption peaks at ∼ 490 and ∼ 549 nm, respectively, were observed. These newly generated absorption peaks indicate the chemical reaction happened between the C-dots and ClO-, resulting in the quenching of green fluorescence. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4B, the much stronger nitryl characteristic.

peak at 1345 cm−1, and new C-Cl peak at 645 cm−1 [62,67], appeared on the FT-IR spectra of the oxidized C-dots. These observations, together with some variations of the peaks from 1730 to 1420 cm−1, suggested that the oxidization of amino groups on the edge of the C-dots into nitryl ones, which might be responsible for the fluorescence quenching because the nitryl group is the common fluorescence quenching one. The XPS result of the oxidized C-dots further verifies this hypothesis. The survey curve of the XPS spectrum reveals that the oxidized C-dots composed of four elements, i.e., carbon (∼285.1 eV), nitrogen (∼399.2 eV), oxygen (∼531.9 eV) and chlorine (∼199.4 eV) (Fig. 4C). In comparison to 9.89% of atomic ratio of oxygen of the raw C-dots, that of the oxidized C-dots reached 20.87%. In addition, the high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 4D displays that the two peaks with the corresponding binding energy located at ∼ 400.1 eV and ∼ 398.9 eV, which could be assigned to –NO2 and C-N, respectively [68]. Theoretically, the amine peak should be in the range of 396–398 eV, which disappeared in the high-resolution N1s spectrum of the oxidized C-dots. These results coincide with the FT-IR results and suggest that the amine was completely oxidized into nitryl. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4E, a new type of covalent bond C-Cl with binding energy at ∼ 199.0 eV was found on the high-resolution Cl2p XPS spectrum [67], and Cl content was measured as 19.83%, manifesting the occurrence of chlorination reaction. Also, this result indicates that the C-dots were oxidized by ClO- to chlorine-doped C-dots (Scheme 1D). The excess of Cl content possibly resulted from the incomplete purification. The fluorescence decay curves of ClO- titrating C-dots aqueous solution were carried out. The results indicate the lifetime of C-dots reduced with the addition of ClO- (Fig. S3). Ascorbic acid can reduce nitryl into amine, causing the recovery of quenched fluorescence of the C-dots [68]. The results shown in Fig. 4F also support this hypothesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of the C-dots in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the C-dots probe for the detection of ClO-, the matrix effects brought by the possible co-existing metal ions and biomolecules were considered under the same conditions. The effects of 1 mM of NH4 +, Na+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, and Ca2+, and 0.5 mM of Zn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ on the fluorescent responses of C-dots were investigated, the result indicates the routine metal ions have no effects on the detection of ClO- (Fig. S4A). Similarly, the effects of biomolecules such as 0.1 μM of calf thymus DNA, BSA, 1 μM of lysine, glutathione, histidine, cysteine, proline, and tyrosine, 10 μM of uric acid, and urea, and 1 mM of glucose on the ClO- detection were also taken into account. The same results as metal ions were given (Fig. S4B). All these results indicate that the present probe exhibited the high selectivity for ClO-. The quantitative recoveries were carried out through the quantificational injection of ClO- into DDW samples. The quantitative recoveries 105.6%, 101.2%, and 96.24% for the DDW spiked with 25, 50, and 80 µM of ClO-, respectively, were obtained, demonstrating the potential applicability of the C-dots for the determination of ClO- in real samples.

To evaluate the practical potentials of the developed sensing platform for the real samples, environmental waste water samples were tested. As listed in Table S2, the detection results of the developed sensing platform were in keeping with the spiked ClO- amounts. Also, the average recoveries (95.84% – 104.96%) for waste water were satisfactory, further demonstrating the great potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly efficient removal of ClO- in real samples by C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to highly efficiently remove the ClO- in real samples, and recycle and regenerate the adsorbent, we firstly prepared and simply characterized the 3-APPA capped Fe3O4 MNPs using FeCl3 and FeSO4 as raw materials under strong basicity (Scheme 1B). The XRD pattern of the Fe3O4 MNPs is in keeping with that of spinel magnetite (JCPDS card no. 75–1610) (Fig. S5A). The FT-IR spectrum of the Fe3O4 MNPs shows a strong Fe-O vibration at 575 cm−1, which is the characteristic of magnetite core. A broad Fe–O–P and P = O stretching vibrations at 975 cm−1 was observed. Moreover, the characteristic peaks at 2965 and 3425 cm−1 can attribute to C-H and –NH2 of 3-APPA, respectively (Fig. S5B). Both above indicated the successful synthesis of positively charged 3-APPA capped Fe3O4 MNPs with + 8.2 mV of zeta-potential (Fig. S6). Also, C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs containing interaction sites with ClO- were fabricated through self-assembly between Fe3O4 MNPs and C-dots. The −1.2 mV of zeta-potential of C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs was found (Fig. S7). The photographs of ultrasonic dispersion and aggregation state by a magnet make clear the formation of C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs (inset in Fig. 5 ). Fig. 5 is the magnetization curves of two magnetic nanomaterials at room temperature, both showing a typical superparamagnetic behavior. The saturation magnetization values of the Fe3O4 MNPs and C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs were found to be 33.46 and 60.08 emu/g, respectively. The reduced saturation magnetization of C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs lies in the weight contribution from the nonmagnetic C-dots, which is, of course, enough for magnetic separation of adsorbent from a solution with a magnet.

On the basis of the reaction activity with ClO- and excellent superparamagnetism of C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs, they were applied to highly efficient removal of ClO- in real samples, and the developed C-dots sensing platform was conducted to determine the residual ClO- in real samples due to the negligible matrix effect. The analytical results are listed in Table S3. It can be seen that when the adsorbent (C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs) concentration was immobilized at 20 mg/mL, the adsorbent reacted with the spiked ClO- for 30 min, and then was removed by a magnet. The residual ClO- in waste water samples can result in no changes of fluorescence intensity of 12 mg/mL of C-dots, indicating the residual ClO- concentration was lower than 0.5 μM, which is the lowest response concentration of C-dots to ClO-, demonstrating the robust ability of C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs for the highly efficient removal of ClO- (Scheme 1C). In particular, different from the routine approach by the addition of reductant to wipe off the ClO-, our proposed C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs are at least capable of partially eliminating the influence of the release of chloride ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lysosome localization of C-dots and intracellular imaging of ClO-
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the excellent photoluminescent performances, easy-to-synthesis, low cost and good stability, the C-dots were expected to apply to image in cells. Prior to the intracellular imaging, MTT viability assay was conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the C-dots using HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. S8, after 24 h incubation, though a much higher concentration of the C-dots resulted in the much lower cell viability, the cell viabilities kept over 84% at the C-dots concentration of 100 μg/mL. This result suggests the feasibility of applying the C-dots for cell imaging.

Given the low cytotoxicity of the C-dots and the negligible matrix effect (Fig. S4B), the fluorescence imaging behavior of the C-dots in HeLa cells was investigated. As shown in Fig. S9, the much brighter lysosome of HeLa cells was observed after the incubation with C-dots for 6 h at 37 °C [69–72]. In contrast, the cell nucleus looked very dark, indicating that the C-dots did not permeate cells [73,74]. These observations indicate the C-dots could be utilized as a potential lysosome localization probe. To confirm the lysosomal specificity of the C-dots, a co-localization experiment was conducted by incubating HeLa cells with C-dots and Lyso-Tracker Red, which is a commercialized probe for the lysosomal imaging. As shown in Fig. 6 , the green fluorescence (Fig. 6A) of the C-dots completely overlapped with the red one (Fig. 6B) of the Lyso-Tracker Red (Fig. 6D). In addition, the Pearson’s coefficient of the merged image was 0.87 for the C-dots. The bright-field transmission of Hela cells exhibited clear cell images (Fig. 6C). These results confirm that the C-dots could permeate the cell membrane and accumulates in the lysosomes, which was possibly due to the amine, nitryl, and hydroxyl structures on the surface of the C-dots (Scheme 1E) [69,75]. Furthermore, with the ClO- being incubated into intracellular lysosome, the green fluorescence of the C-dots was quenched (Fig. 7 B) (Scheme 1F). Other images are similar to the corresponding ones shown in Fig. 6 (Fig. 7A, 7C-E). The effect of dosage of ClO- on cell images gave the same results (Fig. S10). Therefore, the C-dots with the features of non-toxicity and well biocompatibility can serve as an effective probe for the lysosome localization and real-time detection of ClO- in lysosome.

In summary, we fabricated novel green fluorescence C-dots functionalized by amine, nitryl, and hydroxyl, which sensitively and quickly responded to ClO- in aqueous solution through the oxidization of amine to nitryl, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. Unexpectedly, the C-dots can also localize at lysosome, realizing a breakthrough for real-time monitoring and imaging ClO- in cell. Furthermore, C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs were prepared for highly efficient removal of ClO- in environmental samples. In comparison to the conventional dye-based probes, the C-dots as a new lysosome localization probe will greatly simplify the synthesis process. Similarly, as a new adsorbent for the removal of ClO-, C-dots@Fe3O4 MNCs hold much lower release of chloride ions than traditional adsorbent. Undoubtedly, the results from this work will be beneficial to the design and applications of the lysosome-targetable C-dots-based chemosensors and contrast reagent.
